cities.name: Barrington
cities.intro:
Barrington is a Lake County community west of Lake Zurich and north of Hoffman Estates, with a footprint that includes wooded subdivisions, equestrian properties, and rural lots. The terrain was shaped by the last glaciation, which left kettle holes, oak savanna remnants, and patchy heavy clay over deep-buried sand and gravel. North Barrington and the area around Cuba Marsh feature wetland-adjacent properties where drainage and ground-water levels become real factors in landscape and irrigation work. Tower Lake sits within the village footprint and shapes the surrounding microclimate.

The village core preserves a notable concentration of brick farmhouse and traditional New England-style homes, where outdoor lighting and seasonal holiday lighting are visible elements of the streetscape. Many of the larger homes in Barrington and North Barrington were built or expanded in the 1990s, and the brick and flagstone paver patios from that era are now in the window where polymeric re-sanding and edge stabilization commonly come up. Mature plantings and tree canopies through the older subdivisions favor uplighting and pathlighting approaches over driveway-edge strips.

cities.name: Crystal Lake
cities.intro:
Crystal Lake is the largest community in McHenry County, situated around the glacial lake of the same name. The geology shifts noticeably with proximity to the water: properties closest to the lake — through the Crystal Lake South area, around the Park District beach, and into adjacent Lakewood — sit on glacial outwash sand that drains quickly. Further from the lake, in Veterans Acres and the older subdivisions toward the Route 14 corridor, the soil returns to the clay-loam more typical of inland McHenry County. That difference influences irrigation runtime: sandy soils favor frequent, shorter cycles, while clay-loam favors deeper and less frequent watering.

Crystal Lake's housing stock is predominantly 1980s through 2010s mid-size suburban — different in character from the estate-heavy markets along the North Shore. Residential sprinkler systems on quarter- to half-acre lots, holiday lighting on rooflines and front entries, and paver patios and driveway aprons are common features of the local outdoor work landscape. Properties backing the conservation easements and wooded sections of the Park District are well-suited to landscape lighting that emphasizes specimen trees against natural backdrops.

cities.name: Highland Park
cities.intro:
Highland Park is a Lake County community along Lake Michigan, between Lake Forest to the north and Glencoe to the south. The geology mirrors that of Lake Forest: ravines draining east toward the lake, clay-loam soils, and mature oak and elm canopies through the older neighborhoods. Spring frost typically lingers later than in inland Lake County because of Lake Michigan's moderating effect on overnight low temperatures. The Ravinia and Briar Wood areas, and bluff-edge properties along Sheridan Road, sit within the same ravine-shaped terrain that defines the rest of the central North Shore.

The Ravinia neighborhood — anchored by the Ravinia Festival pavilion — sees substantial seasonal traffic between summer and the holiday season, which raises the visibility of outdoor lighting on residences along Lake Cook Road and St. Johns Avenue. Brick and bluestone paver driveways are common throughout the older sections of town and reach the polymeric joint re-sanding and sealing window after the first decade or so of freeze–thaw exposure. Architectural styles range from early-20th-century Tudor and Colonial Revival through mid-century modern, with corresponding variation in outdoor lighting approaches.

cities.name: Lake Forest
cities.intro:
Lake Forest is a Lake County community along the Lake Michigan bluffs, between Lake Bluff to the north and Highwood to the south. The landscape is shaped by ravines that drain east toward the lake, mature oak and elm canopies, and large lots throughout the central and east neighborhoods. Soils tend toward heavy clay-loam, with pockets of poor drainage near the ravine edges that influence both turf health and irrigation zone design. Spring frost typically lingers later in Lake Forest than in inland Lake or McHenry counties because of the moderating effect of Lake Michigan on overnight low temperatures.

Onwentsia, Knollwood, and the bluff-edge properties along Sheridan Road are characterized by estate lots, long driveways, and gated entries — settings where landscape lighting is a substantial part of the local outdoor design vocabulary. The downtown core around Market Square is anchored by Tudor and Georgian architecture, on which traditional holiday lighting is a recognizable seasonal staple. Bluestone walkways and clay-brick paver driveways are common throughout central Lake Forest, particularly in older sections where freeze–thaw cycles eventually call for polymeric joint re-sanding and edge stabilization.

cities.name: Lake Geneva
cities.intro:
Lake Geneva is a Walworth County, Wisconsin community on the north shore of Geneva Lake — a destination market that has historically functioned as a weekend-home community for residents of Chicago and Milwaukee. Lakeshore properties along the south shore through Williams Bay and Fontana, and the homes set off the Geneva Lake Shore Path, sit on glacial outwash with high water tables close to the lake and granular fast-draining soils. Spring frost typically lingers later in this area than in inland Wisconsin or Northern Illinois because of the moderating effect of Geneva Lake on overnight low temperatures.

Wisconsin requires a state plumbing license for irrigation system installation, and lawn sprinkler systems in the state must include a reduced pressure zone (RPZ) backflow assembly. Many properties in the Lake Geneva area function as second homes and benefit from systems designed for owner-absent operation: smart controllers, leak-detection flow sensors, and outdoor lighting on dusk-to-dawn timing. Tudor, shingle-style, and historic estate architecture are concentrated through the Geneva National area and along the lakeshore corridor near Linn Pier — settings where traditional holiday lighting and architectural landscape lighting are a long-standing part of the seasonal landscape.
